evidence.text: Hi! I'm writing a wild west roleplaying campaign. I would like some descriptions of typical old western towns and nature, such as prairies and canyons.

editableNewDialogue.text: If I wanted a more tense vibe for a canyon scene, what little sensory details could I add besides “it’s quiet”?

Use details that feel like the place is listening back: grit crunching under a boot too loudly, a pebble skittering down a slope with a long delay before it clicks to a stop, wind threading through a crack and making a low flute-note, heat shimmer warping the horizon, the sharp mineral smell after a brief sprinkle, a vulture’s wings cutting the air overhead, and echoes that repeat half a second late so every word sounds like it’s being followed.
And for a saloon, how do you show trouble is about to start without immediately having someone draw a gun?

Slow the room down: the piano player misses a note and doesn’t correct it, a laugh dies mid-breath, a chair leg scrapes once and then freezes, cards stop slapping the table, someone’s knuckles go white around a glass, the bartender sets a bottle down a little too carefully, and you notice small rules—hats aren’t being tipped anymore, people stop using names, boots pivot so toes point toward the door.
Random question: was there ever a real train called the “Silver Spur,” or is that just a good fictional name?

It’s a great fictional name. Historically you do see “Silver” show up in mine-to-rail branding (especially in Nevada/Colorado contexts), but “Silver Spur” is more common as a hotel/saloon/ranch name than a famous railroad line. If you want a reality-adjacent option, you could name the service something like “The Silver Limited” run by a fictional regional company, and let locals nickname it “the Spur.”

One more thing—I'm switching our next session to online since the group can't all meet at the table this week. Could you help me sketch a quick "session zero" handout for the Wild West campaign so everyone's on the same page?

Absolutely—here’s a clean, one-page Session Zero handout you can paste into a doc or send as a message. Tell me your preferred tone (gritty, pulpy, comedic) and any hard lines/veils you want, and I’ll tailor it.

Quick correction: I’m not writing a wild west roleplaying campaign anymore—I scrapped that idea. I’m actually putting together a short story set in the same kind of old western town and landscape, so I still need setting details, just for prose instead of a game.
Got it—so no campaign planning now, but you’re writing a short story and want those old-west setting materials (town textures, prairies, canyons, that sort of thing) to support the prose.
